nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names В© Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dД“ вҖҳthighвҖҷ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Folville
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names В© Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish (of Norman origin): habitational name from Colleville in Seine-Maritime, France, named with the Scandinavian personal name Koli (see Cole 2) + Old French ville вҖҳsettlement, villageвҖҷ. The Scottish branch of the family were descended from Philip de Colville, who came to Scotland as a household knight of kings Malcolm IV (1153вҖ“65) and William вҖҳthe LionвҖҷ (1165вҖ“1214).

variants.name: Cole
meanings: English: usually from the Middle English and Old French personal name Col(e), Coll(e), Coul(e), a pet form of Nicol (see Nichol and Nicholas ), a common personal name from the mid 13th century onward. English families with this name migrated to Scotland and to Ulster (especially Fermanagh).

English: occasionally perhaps from a different (early) Middle English personal name Col, of native English or Scandinavian origin. Old English Cola was originally a nickname from Old English col вҖҳcoalвҖҷ in the sense вҖҳcoal-black (of hair), swarthyвҖҷ and is the probable source of most of the examples in Domesday Book. In the northern and eastern counties of England settled by Vikings in the 10th and 11th centuries, alternative sources are Old Norse Kolr and Koli (either from a nickname вҖҳthe swarthy oneвҖҷ or a short form of names in Kol-), and Old Norse Kollr (from a nickname, perhaps вҖҳthe bald oneвҖҷ).
English: nickname for someone with swarthy skin or black hair, from Middle English col, coul(e) вҖҳcharcoal, coalвҖҷ (Old English col).

variants.name: Colvin
meanings: Scottish and Irish (Donegal, Antrim): variant of   Colville  , probably reflecting a local pronunciation. The name was taken to Ulster in the 17th century.
Manx: if not identical with 1, perhaps from the Old Norse personal name Kolbeinn, with /v/ substituted for /b/.

English: from the rare Middle English personal name Colwin, Colvin, which may be a borrowing into English of a Welsh name whose modern form is Collwyn вҖҳwhiteвҖҷ, or of colwyn вҖҳdoe, puppy, pet dogвҖҷ, or of the placename Colwyn (Denbighshire, Radnorshire).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names В© Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil вҖҳwill, desireвҖҷ + helm вҖҳhelmet, protectionвҖҷ. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the вҖҳconqueredвҖҷ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: William De Folville was born as the son of Walter Folville. He had at least 1 son with Miss Eustace De Es. He died after 1236.
